You are generating ONE complete HIGH-LEVEL system design (HLD) interview session for an Uber/Amazon-style senior SDE prep app. FIRST, read these two files IN FULL — they define the exact quality bar and section structure: - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md (the production HLD system prompt — your answer MUST match its §1–§11 structure exactly, with all the prescribed sub-structure, emoji markers (🎬 🎙️ 💬 🗣️ 🧠 🤝 🆘 ⚠️ 🔀 ❓), and the Bad→Good→Great deep dives) - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/hld_session_builder.md (the build recipe; IGNORE its old /Users/anshullkgarg/... paths — the real root is /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora) WHAT HLD IS (vs LLD): NO application/algorithm code, NO classes, NO §1–§9-LLD structure. HLD is prose + tables + mermaid architecture diagrams. The ONLY code that ever appears: (1) realistic JSON request/response examples in the API section, and (2) Python you WRITE to a file and RUN to compute capacity numbers exactly — only the rounded results are surfaced in the markdown, never unrun code. REQUIRED SECTION STRUCTURE (all 11, fully expanded, in this order — match the headings): ## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional) — 🎬 structure pitch opener; 3-5 functional flows "above the line" each with feature one-liner + 🗣️ plain words + ⚡ why it's hard; "Below the line (out of scope)" list; NFRs each with a talkable target (p99 ms, 9s) + 🗣️ plain words + 💥 what breaks without it; close with 🎙️ Script. TEXT ONLY. ## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions — each: spoken question, 🗣️ why I'm asking, ↔️ design fork (If YES.../If NO...), Assumption I'll proceed with; end with 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) — a table: Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives. COMPUTE the arithmetic by writing a capacity.py and running it with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python capacity.py — paste the rounded results. Name the ONE decision-forcing number + its flip threshold. Plain-text math only (~, x, ÷, no LaTeX). End with a short 🎙️ Script. ## 4. Core Entities — per entity: name + what it owns, 🗣️ simple analogy, ⚠️ interviewer probe + one-line answer; 🎙️ Script. ## 5. API / Interface — per functional requirement ~1 endpoint: method+path (+ why this verb), 2-4 key request fields (+ why; call out server-set fields), a concrete JSON request+response example, one key design decision (idempotency key etc.); security note (caller from JWT, never trust client ids/prices). 🎙️ Script. ## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time) — one ### 6.x per above-the-line requirement: one-sentence framing; ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE (Component | plain English | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted); 🗣️ key-terms gloss block; a focused mermaid flowchart TD diagram; NUMBERED STEP NARRATION (action / why this component / state change / what could go wrong); 💬 say-while-drawing line; 🎙️ Script. End with ONE "Final (high-level)" mermaid flowchart TD that is the reconciled union of all slices + 🎙️ Script + 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 7. Data Model & Storage — PART A entity table (Entity | key fields with PK/shard key | chosen store | partition key | consistency); PART B a storage decision card per distinct store (what it is / why this — the access pattern that forces it / considered & rejected / trade-off / 🗣️ how to say it); PART C per-operation consistency table. 🎙️ Script. ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great — 3-5 hardest problems, each "### How do we [problem]?" escalating Bad/Good/Great; each tier: ↩️ what previous got wrong / 🗣️ plain words / Approach (mechanism) / ⚠️ what breaks (with concrete numbers) / 🔁 what forces the upgrade; Good & Great get a tier-specific mechanism mermaid diagram; Great adds 🔢 decision-forcing math + ✅ failure matrix. Each ends with 🎙️ Script + 🧠 "If they ask". Cover the binding bottleneck + relief + new ceiling, hot-key/celebrity, idempotency, any global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, failure behavior. ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost — 9A availability (9s per path + human downtime + mechanisms); 9B per-dependency failure table (Component | what breaks | how it degrades gracefully — what USER SEES | recovery); 9C RPO/RTO (define both in plain words first, then table per data class); 9D cost (top 3 drivers + why + rough monthly order-of-magnitude + the one biggest optimization). 🎙️ Script. ## 10. Trade-off Ledger — 2-4 decision cards "🔀 Decision: [chose] vs [didn't]": what we chose & why / what we gave up / 🗣️ plain words / when this reverses (exact flip condition) / 🗣️ how to say it. 🎙️ Script. ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers — all 7 domains (core algorithm/hardest part, failure, scale/hot-spot, consistency, security, cost, extensibility), 2-3 each, 12-18 total; each: ❓ question verbatim, the mechanism (2-3 sentences, terms glossed), 🗣️ in plain words, 💬 one-liner to say. End with a 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary. MERMAID RULES (a real renderer parses these — get them right; HLD has MANY diagrams so this matters a lot): - Use ```mermaid fenced blocks. DEFAULT to "flowchart TD" for architecture/multi-path (NEVER flowchart LR for multi-path — it stretches off screen; LR only for a trivial 3-4 node linear chain). Use sequenceDiagram for request flows. - KEEP NODE/EDGE LABELS FREE of these breaking characters: ( ) [ ] | : / " < > ; and curly braces { }. A semicolon ';' or colon ':' or parens inside a label/message BREAKS the parser. Write "step 3 read cache" not "3: read cache (hot)". Use
for line breaks inside a node, never a raw newline. - Edge labels: short, like -->|"read leaderboard"| (text inside the pipes must itself avoid the breaking chars above — no ':' or ';' or parens inside it). - Same component = same node id/label across every diagram. Cap ~10-12 nodes per diagram. - Each §6.x slice gets its own diagram; §6 ends with one "Final (high-level)" union diagram; deep-dive Good/Great tiers get mechanism diagrams. Aim for 5-9 mermaid blocks total. MARKDOWN OUTPUT: - Write the COMPLETE §1–§11 markdown to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/_response.md . It must be LONG and comprehensive (the md must be > 20,000 chars; realistically 40-55K). Pure markdown — tables with real line breaks, fenced code only for the JSON API examples and mermaid. No LaTeX. Emoji markers as prescribed. - You MAY create a scratch workspace dir /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/ to write capacity.py and run it with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python. Do NOT write into /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/ — the orchestrator assembles the JSON afterwards. Before finishing: (1) verify capacity.py actually runs and you used its real computed numbers; (2) self-check that all 11 section headings are present and the md is > 20K chars; (3) count your ```mermaid blocks and make sure each one's labels are free of the breaking characters listed above. === YOUR ASSIGNED HLD PROBLEM === UUID: 75db5204-414d-4c67-a406-d744f3acb6d4 TITLE: Design Nearby Places / Proximity Service (Yelp/Tinder-style) PROBLEM BRIEF (this is what transcript[0] will say; frame §1 around it): A proximity/geo service: given a user location, return nearby places (or people) within a radius, ranked. Focus on geo-indexing (geohash/quadtree/S2), the read path at scale, and updating moving entities. WHAT TO EMPHASIZE (capacity numbers, the binding bottleneck, and the deep dives should center on these — this is what makes the session strong): geo-indexing options (geohash vs quadtree vs S2 cell) and why, radius/k-nearest query on the index, sharding by geo-cell + hot dense cells (cities) as the hot-key problem, caching popular queries, updating location for moving entities (Tinder/driver case). Deep dives: geo-index choice + query, hot dense cell (city) problem, moving-entity location updates, ranking + read scaling. Assume an Amazon/Uber senior-SDE (SDE-3 / L5) bar: cost & operations are first-class, work backwards from the customer, decide and move. Build the complete §1–§11 HLD now in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/75db5204-414d-4c67-a406-d744f3acb6d4_hld/_response.md . Read the two prompt files first, write+run capacity.py for the numbers, then write the full markdown. Return the structured summary (mdChars = actual char count, mermaidCount = actual count of ```mermaid blocks, sectionsPresent = all 11 "## N." headings present).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1 2 You are a world-class System Design (High Level Design) interview coach and Principal Engineer. The user is in (or preparing for) a LIVE system-design interview and will give a problem (e.g. "design a URL shortener", "design Twitter/News Feed", "design a ride-sharing backend"), as text or an image. Produce ONE complete, teachable design the candidate can deeply understand AND narrate out loud — covering, in a single pass, everything a senior interviewer might probe. 3 4 5 6 The candidate is preparing for THIS specific interview. Tailor your framing and emphasis to its company, role, values, and tech focus; where it fits naturally, lean on the candidate's listed real experience. (Behavioural answer: map to the values and use the mapped stories. System-design answer: favor the role's tech focus — e.g. Kafka for an eventing role.) 7 # Target Interview — Amazon (SDE-3) 8 9 > The specific round Anshul is preparing for right now. API / Interface 335 Go one-by-one through the functional requirements and define the endpoint(s) that satisfy each — usually 1:1. For EACH endpoint write: 336 - **Method + path** — and one sentence on WHY this HTTP verb (POST creates new state, GET only reads, DELETE removes, PUT replaces fully). This should be something the candidate can say out loud. 337 - **Key request fields** (NOT a full schema — just the 2-4 fields that matter most): `fieldName: type` — one line WHY it exists (e.g. "`paymentMethodId: string` — the token from our payment processor; we send this ID, never the raw card number, so we're out of PCI scope"). If a field is intentionally ABSENT (e.g. price, userId), say WHY — "the client does NOT send the price; the server fetches it from the DB so no one can hack the checkout price by editing the request". 338 - **Concrete example** — write a small JSON block showing a real example request body (for POST/PUT) and the corresponding response body. Use realistic values, not "string" placeholders. For GET endpoints, show the query parameters as a URL + the response JSON. This is what the candidate draws on the whiteboard to make the API tangible. Example format: 339 ``` 340 // Request 341 POST /v1/orders 342 { "paymentMethodId": "pm_abc123", "cartId": "cart_xyz789" } 343 344 // Response 200 345 { "orderId": "ord_def456", "status": "PENDING", "estimatedAt": "2025-01-15T14:32:00Z" } 346 ``` 347 Include only the fields that matter; omit verbose boilerplate. If an important field is SERVER-SET (never in the request), show it only in the response and call it out: "notice `status` is absent from the request — the server initializes it; client can't forge state." 348 - **One key design decision** on this endpoint — the thing you'd call out in an interview: idempotency key, why PENDING not synchronous, why a redirect vs a JSON response, etc. State it in plain words. 349 Default REST unless there's a reason not to; call out and justify non-obvious choices. SECURITY: identify the caller from session/JWT, never trust client-supplied ids/timestamps/prices — explain this in plain words the candidate can say: "the price comes from our DB at checkout time, not from the request, so the client can't manipulate it." 💬 note + a 🎙️ Script walking the endpoints. 350 351 ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) 352 Build ONE subsection per above-the-line requirement from §1 — cover ALL of them, in priority order, start minimal, don't jump to scaling. CRITICAL: every §6.x subsection you open gets its OWN focused diagram + numbered narration — do NOT trail off after a few, do NOT fold a real flow into a single step of an earlier diagram, and do NOT leave a diagram-less text-only "this just reuses the above" stub. If a flow mostly reuses existing components, STILL draw the cumulative diagram and highlight the new edges/state it adds (e.g. the accept/decline transition, the navigation/track stream). A flow either earns a full diagrammed subsection here or it stays out of §6 — never a half-baked one. For EACH requirement, a subsection: 353 ### 6.x "" 354 - A one-sentence framing of this slice — what the user is trying to do and why this flow is interesting. 355 - Introduce ONLY the new components this slice needs, wired onto what already exists. 356 357 **ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE** — For EACH new component, write FIVE columns: **Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept** The "What it is" column must define the technology in one plain sentence as if explaining to a smart non-engineer — NO jargon without definition. The "Why THIS choice" column must name the specific reason this problem needs this tool (not generic "it scales" — something precise like "ES because we need ranked full-text search across 100M products in <100ms — no SQL can do that"). The "What we DIDN'T pick" column names 1-2 realistic alternatives and one concrete reason each was rejected (e.g. "Not SQL LIKE: would scan every product row on every search — 100M rows × every query = unusable"; "Not a custom inverted index: months of engineering with no relevance ranking"). Keep this to 2–5 rows per slice. Example format: 358 | Search index | Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds a map of words → products, so a query returns matches in ms | Need ranked full-text search across 100M products in under 100ms | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row, no ranking); not a hand-rolled index (months of work) | Lags behind catalog DB by a few seconds (fine for display) | 359 THEN: a 🎙️ NARRATION block (not the table — this is what the candidate says OUT LOUD) that speaks each choice conversationally: define the technology briefly, say WHY you picked it, say what you considered instead and discarded, and state the trade-off you accepted. One short paragraph per component. The candidate reads this and should be able to say it naturally without memorizing jargon. 360 361 **GLOSSES BLOCK** — After the table, write a dedicated "**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**" block. For EVERY technical term used in the table or this section (a service name, a pattern name, a protocol), write it as a standalone bolded entry with a plain-English definition + a one-line "why it matters here" sentence. Format: 362 > **Elasticsearch** — [what it is in one simple sentence]. Here we use it because [one line]. 363 > **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 364 > **Kafka** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 365 Each entry must be self-contained — the candidate can read just that entry and understand the term. Never bundle multiple terms into one paragraph; each gets its own line. 366 367 **DIAGRAM** — Include a diagram for this slice ONLY if it adds clarity that text alone cannot provide (a new component, a non-obvious data path, a branching flow). If this slice only adds one edge to existing components already fully drawn in a previous subsection, SKIP the diagram and write "↳ builds on §6.x diagram — no new components" instead. When you DO include one: 368 - Use ` ```mermaid flowchart TD ` (top-down) by DEFAULT — this keeps the diagram narrow enough to fit a 13-inch MacBook Air screen without horizontal scrolling. Switch to `flowchart LR` ONLY for a dead-simple 3-4 node linear sequence (A→B→C, no branching) where vertical space would be wasted. 369 - Use `subgraph` blocks to separate conceptually distinct paths (e.g. subgraph "Read Path" vs subgraph "Write / Sync Path"), with a readable title label on each subgraph. 370 - Keep FOCUSED — only the components and edges this flow touches (~8-12 nodes total). 371 - Color palette strictly: clients (blue #4A90D9 fill), our services (green #2E7D4F fill), async/queues (amber #B7791F fill), datastores as database[] cylinders (grey #4A5568 fill), external (purple #6B46C1 fill). ALL node fills must be dark enough to contrast with white text. 372 - EVERY edge must have a label — not just a number, but a short description: `-->|"1: GET /search"|` not just `-->|1|`. Include WHAT is being sent or what the operation is. 373 - Number the request arrows sequentially 1:1 with the prose narration below. 374 - Add a one-line caption above the diagram (bold) naming the flow. 375 - Directly below the diagram, a one-line `↳ reuses existing: (from §6.x)` note. 376 377 **NUMBERED STEP NARRATION** — Walk through each numbered arrow in the diagram. For EACH step write: 378 1. What happens (the action) — in a complete sentence. 379 2. WHY this component handles it (not just "it goes there" but "because this service owns X"). 380 3. What STATE changes (what is different in the system after this step vs before). 381 4. What could go wrong here and how the system handles it (one line — the "what if" every interviewer asks about). 382 This is the richest part of each slice. The candidate should be able to narrate every arrow on the diagram confidently. 383 384 **💬 Say-while-drawing line** — one sentence that captures the KEY insight of this slice, phrased to say out loud while pointing at the diagram. Should name the core tension and the design decision that resolves it. 385 386 **🎙️ Script** — 3–5 connected sentences narrating this slice end-to-end in plain English. Should sound like someone explaining to a colleague, not reading a textbook. Must: define any jargon the first time it appears, state the one architectural decision that makes this slice work, name the trade-off and why it's acceptable. 387 388 End with the ONE full diagram captioned "Final (high-level)" — it MUST be the exact RECONCILED UNION of all the slices: EVERY component that appeared in ANY slice (6.1, 6.2, …) is present here with its same name and color (NOTHING dropped — never lose a cache, DB, or queue that a slice introduced), and it introduces NOTHING that wasn't built in a slice. Use subgraphs in the final diagram to group the "Read Path" components vs "Write / Transaction Path" vs "Async / Events" path so the visual tells the story at a glance. Before finalizing, mentally diff it against the slices and confirm nothing is missing. Annotate load-bearing schema columns inline next to the relevant datastore, then a 🎙️ Script narrating the full flow (2–3 sentences per path, not one blob) and a 🤝 Checkpoint handing the deep-dive choice to the interviewer. 389 390 ## 7. Data Model & Storage 391 **PART A — ENTITY TABLE:** For each entity, one row with: Entity | Key fields (name + type, annotate the PK/shard key) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency guarantee Keep field lists tight — only the columns that matter for access patterns or the deep dives. 392 393 **PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS:** After the table, for EACH distinct storage choice (each different database/store used), write a standalone "decision card" block. Each card has FIVE parts: 394 395 **🗄️ [Store name] — used for: [entities]** 396 1. **What it is (plain words):** one sentence defining this store as if explaining to a non-engineer. (e.g. "PostgreSQL is a relational database — it stores data in rows and columns with strict rules, and it guarantees that a multi-step operation either fully succeeds or fully rolls back.") 397 2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice:** Be specific — name the EXACT reason this problem needs THIS store. Not "it scales" but "we need an atomic 'decrement-and-check' operation that prevents overselling, and only a relational DB with row-level locking can guarantee that two buyers racing for the last unit can't both win." 398 3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:** List 2-3 realistic alternatives with a one-line rejection reason each. Make the rejections concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "DynamoDB would work for the cart but NOT for inventory — it can't do the conditional atomic decrement we need to prevent overselling without a lot of custom application-side logic." E.g.: 399 - **MongoDB** — flexible schema is nice, but we need ACID multi-row transactions for the order+inventory+payment saga; Mongo's transactions are slower and less battle-tested here. 400 - **DynamoDB** — great for carts (KV by user) but no native conditional decrement strong enough for inventory; also vendor lock-in. 401 - **MySQL** — identical to Postgres for this use case; Postgres has better JSON support and is the standard choice in modern stacks. 402 4. **Trade-off we accept:** one line on what we're giving up (e.g. "operational complexity of managing shards; query joins across shards are impossible so we denormalize"). 403 5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** 2-3 natural sentences the candidate can say verbatim — casual, not textbook. (e.g. "For orders and inventory I'm using Postgres, because this is money and I can't afford a half-committed transaction. I looked at Dynamo — it's great for the cart — but it can't do the atomic stock decrement I need without extra complexity. The trade-off is I have to shard manually, but that's fine because I shard by order_id and each shard is independent.") 404 405 **PART C — Per-operation consistency summary:** one compact table: Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here Cover: the writes that MUST be strong (money, inventory), the reads that can be eventual (display, search), and the read-your-own-writes edge case. 406 407 **🎙️ Script:** 4-6 sentences walking the whole storage tier conversationally — mention the dominant choice (usually Postgres for the core), the KV store, the cache, and the event log. Must name one "I considered X but rejected it because Y" to signal senior-level thinking. 408 409 ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great (the senior signal) 410 This is where the interview is won. Start with a one-line 🆘 "If you get stuck" recovery pointer. Pick the 3–5 hardest problems (driven by the non-functional requirements and edge cases). Pose EACH as a question header: **"### How do we [problem]?"** 411 412 For EACH deep dive, escalate through tiers. Each tier has a FIXED structure — follow it exactly: 413 414 **#### [Bad / Good / Great]: ** 415 416 *(For Good and Great tiers only)* **↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Open with one sentence saying exactly what broke in the tier before and WHY this new approach fixes that specific thing. This makes the progression feel like logical problem-solving, not a random list of techniques. E.g. for Good after Bad: "Bad held the lock across the payment call — so I split it: reserve first in a millisecond, pay second, outside any lock." Never skip this line in Good or Great. 417 418 **🗣️ What this is in plain words:** One sentence explaining the approach as if to a smart non-engineer who has never heard of it. No jargon without definition. This line comes FIRST, before any technical description, so the candidate understands what they're about to describe. 419 420 **Approach:** Explain what you actually do in this tier — the mechanism. Be specific about the steps. Every technical term used here must be defined inline the FIRST time it appears in this deep dive (not just in the glosses — right here, parenthetically: e.g. "a row lock (a database mechanism that prevents any other transaction from reading or writing that row until you release it)"). 421 422 **Why this seemed reasonable / Why people try this:** One line — the intuition behind this approach. The candidate should be able to say "I tried X first because it felt natural — it's what most people reach for." 423 424 **⚠️ What breaks (Challenges):** Be concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "at 500K buyers racing for one item during a flash sale, the lock is held across a payment call that takes 300ms, so 500K threads are now waiting — the database connection pool (the fixed set of open connections the app keeps — typically 50-200) exhausts in seconds, and the whole site stops responding." Name the numbers. Name the failure mode. Name why this matters for THIS system. 425 426 **🔁 What forces the upgrade to the next tier:** one sentence — what exact scenario makes this tier break that the next tier fixes. (Omit on the final Great tier — replace with the failure matrix or trade-offs instead.) 427 428 Tiers are OPTIONAL — skip Bad if the answer is obvious; don't manufacture tiers. For EACH MEANINGFUL tier (usually Good and Great), include a titled mermaid diagram showing ONLY the mechanism for that tier — not the whole system. For the Great tier also include: 429 430 **🔢 Decision-forcing math (if applicable):** Show a quick calculation that proves why this tier matters — e.g. "500K buyers × 300ms lock hold ÷ 200 connection pool = exhaustion in under 1 second." Keep it rounded and talkable. 431 432 **✅ Failure matrix (for correctness-critical deep dives):** A compact table of every failure scenario and what happens — e.g. "Payment succeeds but commit crashes → reconcile by idempotency key → complete commit, no double-charge." Cover: success, failure, timeout, retry, partial failure, crash-after-payment. 433 434 Each deep dive ends with: 435 - **🎙️ Script:** 3-5 connected sentences saying the Great solution out loud, in plain conversational English. Define every term the first time. Sound like an engineer explaining to a colleague, not reading slides. 436 - **🧠 "If they ask…":** the single most obvious pushback and a 2-sentence answer. 437 438 Cover where relevant: the binding bottleneck + its relief + the new ceiling after the fix; hot-key / celebrity-item problems; idempotency and duplicate suppression; every global coordinator (anything that is a single point of serialization); consistency stance (strong vs eventual) and read-your-own-writes; what happens when each piece fails. 439 440 ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost 441 Structure this as FOUR named sub-sections, each with plain-English definitions for every term: 442 443 **9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):** 444 For each path (read path, money/write path), state the availability target as "X nines" AND what that means in human terms: "four nines = 52 minutes of downtime per year total; three nines = 8.7 hours." Then state the mechanisms that achieve it (CDN, replicas, multi-AZ failover). Make the mechanism readable: define "multi-AZ" (multiple data centres in the same region so if one building loses power the other takes over automatically) the first time it appears. 445 446 **9B — Per-dependency failure table:** 447 For EACH major component, one row: Component | What breaks if it goes down | How the system degrades gracefully | Recovery action. The "graceful degradation" column must describe what the USER SEES — not just "falls back to replica" but "users can still browse categories but search results might be slightly stale." Define every fallback mechanism inline (e.g. "serve from Redis cache (the in-memory store) until the search cluster recovers"). 448 449 **9C — RPO / RTO per data class (plain definitions FIRST):** 450 Define RPO and RTO in plain words before using them: "RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if a crash happens right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is synchronously replicated before we acknowledge it. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long before the service is working again after a crash." Then give a table: Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism. Cover: orders/payments (RPO≈0), catalog (looser — can rebuild from source), search index (rebuildable, minutes of lag OK). 451 452 **9D — Cost breakdown:** 453 Name the top 3 cost drivers and WHY each is expensive in plain terms (e.g. "CDN egress — every byte of image data served to a user's browser is charged by the CDN; at millions of page views a day this is the biggest line item, not the database"). Give a rough monthly order-of-magnitude. Name the one optimization that would cut cost the most. 454 455 **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences on the failure story — what the user EXPERIENCES when each major thing fails, not just what the system does internally. 456 457 ## 10. Trade-off Ledger 458 For EACH of the 2–4 most significant design decisions, write a DECISION CARD with five parts: 459 460 **🔀 Decision: [what you chose] vs [what you didn't]** 461 1. **What we chose and why:** one line — the core reason. 462 2. **What we gave up:** one concrete thing that's harder or worse because of this choice. 463 3. **🗣️ Plain words:** one sentence a non-engineer can understand — "think of it like choosing a fast highway that's slightly less reliable over a slower but guaranteed road." 464 4. **When this reverses:** the exact condition that would make you flip this decision — be specific ("if flash sales are removed from scope, we drop the Redis counter and go back to a single Postgres row — simpler, no reconciliation needed"; "if we owned the payment processor in-house, we could do a real two-phase commit instead of a saga"). 465 5. **🗣️ How to say it:** one sentence the candidate can say out loud — natural, not textbook. 466 467 Tie each decision back to the non-functional requirements and any "⚠️ Gaps" from §1. **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences walking the ledger — the decisions, the trade-offs, and what would make you change your mind. 468 469 ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers 470 Cover ALL of these question domains — generate 2-3 questions per domain, 12-18 total: (1) Core algorithm / the hardest part of THIS specific problem (e.g. "two buyers race for the last unit — who wins?"), (2) Failure scenarios ("what if the payment processor goes down?", "what if Redis crashes?"), (3) Scale / hot-spot ("how do you handle a flash sale?"), (4) Consistency / correctness ("can the same order be placed twice?", "can you oversell?"), (5) Security ("how do you stop price tampering?", "what if someone forges a user id?"), (6) Cost ("what's the most expensive part?", "how would you cut costs?"), (7) Extensibility ("how would you add feature X?", "how would you support Y?"). Do NOT generate only checkout/core questions — a real interview covers all domains. 471 472 For EACH question, write FOUR parts: 473 474 **❓ [The question exactly as an interviewer would say it]** 475 476 **The mechanism (what actually happens):** 2-3 sentences. Every term used that hasn't been defined earlier must be defined inline here. 477 478 **🗣️ In plain words:** 1-2 sentences — same answer, zero jargon. This is what the candidate says if they blank on the technical version: "In simple terms, [plain version]." 479 480 **💬 One-liner to say out loud:** the sharpest single sentence — confident, not textbook. 481 482 Answers must be REAL and SPECIFIC — not "we use caching" but "the product page is in Redis with a 60-second TTL; during a flash sale the cache absorbs ~8K requests/s so Postgres never sees the spike." No one-liners as the main answer. Mechanism + plain words + one-liner every time. 483 484 End with a **🎙️ "60-second verbal summary"** — follow this template exactly, filling in the specifics for this problem: "[1 sentence: what the system is in plain words]. The design splits into two halves. [2-3 sentences: the READ HALF — what it is, what technologies, why it works]. [2-3 sentences: the WRITE / TRANSACTION HALF — what it is, the hardest problem in it, and the one mechanism that solves it]. [1 sentence: how the two halves connect — usually an event stream or async pipeline]. [1 sentence: the single design principle that every hard decision traces back to]." Write this as flowing connected sentences — no bullet points, no section headers. It should sound like someone giving a confident wrap-up to a colleague, not reading notes. 485 486

content.text: I have a thorough understanding of both files. Now let me set up the workspace and write the capacity.py script to compute the numbers.

input.description: Create workspace dir and verify venv python exists

content.text: Now let me write the capacity.py script with the geo-proximity service math.

input.content:
"""
Capacity math for a Nearby Places / Proximity Service (Yelp/Tinder/driver-style).
We compute only the numbers that DRIVE a design decision, rounded to talkable phrases.
Run: /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python capacity.py
"""

def human(n):
    for unit, div in [("T", 1e12), ("B", 1e9), ("M", 1e6), ("K", 1e3)]:
        if abs(n) >= div:
            return f"{n/div:.1f}{unit}"
    return f"{n:.0f}"

SEC_PER_DAY = 86_400

print("=" * 70)
print("PROXIMITY SERVICE — CAPACITY")
print("=" * 70)

# ---------- 1. Users & DAU ----------
total_users = 300_000_000          # 300M registered (Yelp-ish global)
dau_frac = 0.10                    # 10% open the app on a given day
dau = total_users * dau_frac
print(f"\n[Users] total={human(total_users)}  DAU={human(dau)}")

# ---------- 2. Nearby-search READ QPS ----------
searches_per_dau = 5               # avg nearby searches per active user per day
total_searches = dau * searches_per_dau
read_qps_avg = total_searches / SEC_PER_DAY
peak_factor = 4                    # commute / lunch / Friday-night spike
read_qps_peak = read_qps_avg * peak_factor
print(f"\n[Read] searches/day={human(total_searches)}")
print(f"       avg read QPS = {total_searches:,.0f} / {SEC_PER_DAY} = {read_qps_avg:,.0f}/s  (~{human(read_qps_avg)}/s)")
print(f"       peak read QPS = {read_qps_avg:,.0f} x {peak_factor} = {read_qps_peak:,.0f}/s  (~{human(read_qps_peak)}/s)")

# ---------- 3. Cache: miss QPS that actually hits the geo-index ----------
hit_ratio = 0.80                   # popular (city, category, radius) tuples cached
miss_qps_peak = read_qps_peak * (1 - hit_ratio)
print(f"\n[Cache] hit ratio assumed = {hit_ratio:.0%}")
print(f"        peak MISS QPS to geo-index = {read_qps_peak:,.0f} x {1-hit_ratio:.2f} = {miss_qps_peak:,.0f}/s  (~{human(miss_qps_peak)}/s)")
print(f"        cold cache (0% hit) worst case = {read_qps_peak:,.0f}/s  (~{human(read_qps_peak)}/s)")

# ---------- 4. Read replica sizing from MISS QPS ----------
replica_capacity = 8_000           # one geo-index read replica ~8K complex range scans/s
replicas_needed = -(-int(miss_qps_peak) // replica_capacity)  # ceil
cold_replicas = -(-int(read_qps_peak) // replica_capacity)
print(f"\n[Replicas] one replica ~{human(replica_capacity)} range-scan ops/s")
print(f"           warm: ceil({miss_qps_peak:,.0f}/{replica_capacity}) = {replicas_needed} replicas")
print(f"           cold-cache: ceil({read_qps_peak:,.0f}/{replica_capacity}) = {cold_replicas} replicas")

# ---------- 5. MOVING-ENTITY location update WRITE QPS (the driver/Tinder case) ----------
moving_entities = 3_000_000        # 3M active drivers/people sharing live location
update_interval_s = 4              # ping location every 4 seconds while active
write_qps = moving_entities / update_interval_s
print(f"\n[Writes] moving entities online = {human(moving_entities)}")
print(f"         location-update QPS = {moving_entities:,} / {update_interval_s}s = {write_qps:,.0f}/s  (~{human(write_qps)}/s)")

# This is the SAME order of magnitude as peak reads -> write path is first-class.
print(f"         write:read(peak) ratio = {write_qps/read_qps_peak:.2f} : 1")

# ---------- 6. THE decision-forcing number: hottest single geo-cell QPS ----------
# Shard by geo-cell. A dense city core (e.g. Manhattan / SF FiDi) concentrates traffic.
hot_cell_traffic_frac = 0.03       # one dense city cell can pull ~3% of global reads at peak
hot_cell_qps = read_qps_peak * hot_cell_traffic_frac
single_shard_ceiling = 50_000      # one DB/Redis shard tops out ~50K ops/s
print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("DECISION-FORCING NUMBER — hot dense-cell (city) QPS on ONE shard")
print("=" * 70)
print(f"  peak reads x hot-cell fraction = {read_qps_peak:,.0f} x {hot_cell_traffic_frac:.2f}")
print(f"  = {hot_cell_qps:,.0f}/s on the single shard owning that cell  (~{human(hot_cell_qps)}/s)")
print(f"  single-shard ceiling ~{human(single_shard_ceiling)}/s")
print(f"  FLIP THRESHOLD: when one cell's QPS > {human(single_shard_ceiling)}/s -> cannot")
print(f"  live on one shard; must split the cell into finer sub-cells + cache + read replicas.")
print(f"  hot-cell / ceiling = {hot_cell_qps/single_shard_ceiling:.1f}x over a single shard")

# ---------- 7. Storage footprint (non-decision, sanity only) ----------
places = 50_000_000                # 50M static places (Yelp-style)
bytes_per_place = 1_000            # row + geo-index entry + secondary indexes
place_bytes = places * bytes_per_place
# Moving-entity live location: kept hot in memory, not durable-heavy
loc_bytes_per = 100                # lat/lng/ts/entity_id in memory
live_loc_bytes = moving_entities * loc_bytes_per
repl = 3
place_total = place_bytes * repl
print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("STORAGE (sanity, not decision-driving)")
print("=" * 70)
print(f"  static places: {human(places)} x {bytes_per_place}B x {repl} = {human(place_total)}B  (~{place_total/1e12:.1f} TB)")
print(f"  live locations (in-mem): {human(moving_entities)} x {loc_bytes_per}B = {human(live_loc_bytes)}B  (~{live_loc_bytes/1e9:.1f} GB) -> fits in RAM")

# ---------- 8. Rough monthly cost order-of-magnitude ----------
# Dominant drivers: (a) the always-on location-write ingestion fleet + hot geo cache RAM,
# (b) read replica fleet, (c) egress.
print("\n" + "=" * 70)
print("COST (order-of-magnitude, monthly)")
print("=" * 70)
cache_ram_gb = 200                 # hot popular-query + live-location cache
cache_cost = cache_ram_gb * 25     # ~$25/GB-mo managed in-mem
replica_cost = (replicas_needed + cold_replicas) * 1_500  # ~$1.5K/node-mo
ingest_nodes = -(-int(write_qps) // 20_000)  # ceil, ~20K writes/s per stateless ingest node
ingest_cost = ingest_nodes * 800
total_cost = cache_cost + replica_cost + ingest_cost
print(f"  hot in-mem geo cache: {cache_ram_gb}GB x $25 = ${human(cache_cost)}/mo")
print(f"  geo-index replicas: {replicas_needed+cold_replicas} x $1.5K = ${human(replica_cost)}/mo")
print(f"  location-ingest fleet: {ingest_nodes} x $800 = ${human(ingest_cost)}/mo")
print(f"  ~ TOTAL order-of-magnitude = ${human(total_cost)}/mo  (low six figures)")

print("\nDONE.")

input.content:
# Design Nearby Places / Proximity Service (Yelp / Tinder / driver-style)

> 🎙️ **Opener:** "So the problem is a proximity service: a user is somewhere on the map, and they want the nearby things — restaurants, shops, or other people — within some radius, ranked by distance and relevance, fast. The whole game here is the *geo-index* — how we lay points out on the Earth so a 'who's within 2km of me' query doesn't scan the planet — and the *read path at scale*, plus the twist that some of those points are *moving* (drivers, people on a dating app pinging their location every few seconds). I'll target an Uber/Amazon senior bar — real-time, cost-and-ops first-class, decide and move. Tell me if you'd rather I lean Yelp-static-places or Uber-moving-drivers and I'll re-tilt; I'll design for both and call out where they diverge."

> 🎙️ **Structure pitch:** "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and data model, draw the high-level design one requirement at a time, then go deep on the three or four hardest parts — the geo-index choice, the hot dense-city cell, moving-entity updates, and read scaling — checking in with you as I go. Sound good?"

---

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

The core tension: a naive "distance from me to every point" query is O(N) over tens of millions of points and dies instantly. Everything in this design exists to turn that into a bounded lookup — and to keep that lookup fast even when the points are *moving* and the traffic is *lumpy* (dense cities versus empty countryside).

### Functional requirements (the core flows — "above the line")

**FR1 — Search nearby places/entities within a radius.** Given my (lat, lng) and a radius (or "k nearest"), return the points inside that circle.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "I'm standing here, show me the coffee shops within a kilometre, closest first."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** we can't compute distance to all 50M points per request — we need a spatial index that lets us touch only the handful of map cells near the user.

**FR2 — Rank the results.** The returned points come back ordered — by distance, and for places also by rating/popularity/open-now; for people by match score.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Don't just dump 200 places on me — put the good, close, open-now ones at the top."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** ranking needs *more* than the geo-index can hold (ratings, popularity, business hours), so we hydrate candidates from another store under the same latency budget.

**FR3 — Update the location of a moving entity.** A driver or a dating-app user pings their current position every few seconds; the index must reflect it quickly.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "The blue dots on the map are people moving around — keep them current so 'nearby' actually means nearby *now*."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** this is a write *firehose* — millions of moving entities pinging every few seconds is far more writes than reads, and each ping may move a point across a cell boundary, re-bucketing it.

**FR4 — Register / update a static place.** A business is created or edits its location, hours, category.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "A new restaurant opens — put it on the map so people can find it."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** low write volume but it must propagate into the geo-index and the ranking store consistently, without a stale "moved" pin.

**Below the line (out of scope for today):** auth/signup, the recommendation/feed engine, reviews and photos storage, payments/booking, turn-by-turn navigation, abuse/spam moderation, the map-tile rendering itself. I'll mention security and PII where they touch the design but won't build them.

### Non-Functional requirements

**NFR1 — Low read latency: p99 < 200ms for a nearby search.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "99 out of 100 searches feel instant — under a fifth of a second."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the map feels laggy, users re-query, and the read fleet load multiplies on retries.

**NFR2 — Freshness of moving entities: a location ping is visible to searchers within ~5 seconds.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When a driver moves a block, riders see it within a few seconds, not a minute."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** Uber dispatches a driver who's already three blocks past you; Tinder shows someone who left the bar.

**NFR3 — High read availability: 99.99% (four nines) on the search path.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "The 'find nearby' button works essentially always — under ~52 minutes of downtime a year."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the core product (discovery) is dead; this is the revenue path.

**NFR4 — Elastic to geographic skew (hot cities).**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Manhattan at lunch shouldn't melt while Wyoming sits idle."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** one over-loaded city shard becomes a single point of failure that takes down search for that whole metro.

**NFR5 — Cost-efficient: a write-heavy always-on system shouldn't cost a fortune.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "We're paying for millions of location pings every second forever — keep that bill sane."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** the location-ingest fleet quietly becomes the biggest line item and margins evaporate.

> 💬 "The headline tension is this: the *read* shape is 'don't scan the planet' and the *write* shape is 'absorb a firehose of moving dots' — and they fight, because the index that's cheap to read is expensive to keep current under constant motion."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Functionally there are four flows: search nearby within a radius, rank what comes back, update a moving entity's location, and register a static place. The non-functionals that actually shape the design are read latency under 200ms at p99, freshness of moving dots within about five seconds, four-nines availability on the read path, and — the one people forget — graceful behaviour under geographic skew, because traffic clusters in dense cities. I'm explicitly leaving out auth, reviews, navigation, and the recommendation engine so I can spend the time on the geo-index and the read path, which is where this problem lives."

---

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — "Are the entities mostly static places, like Yelp, or mostly moving, like drivers on Uber and people on Tinder?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "If everything's static I can index once and barely ever write; if things move, the write path becomes the hardest part of the whole system, so I want to know where to spend my budget."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If STATIC → a periodically-rebuilt geo-index, writes are rare, optimize purely for reads. If MOVING → a write firehose, an in-memory hot index, and re-bucketing on cell crossings dominates.
- **Assumption:** mixed, but I'll design for the harder *moving* case (Uber/Tinder) and note where static-only would simplify.

**Q2 — "Is the query a fixed radius, or k-nearest-neighbours regardless of distance?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Radius and k-nearest need slightly different index behaviour — radius is a bounded box scan, k-nearest may have to expand the search ring outward until it collects k."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If RADIUS → scan the covering cells, filter by exact distance, done. If K-NEAREST → start at the user's cell and expand to neighbour rings until k candidates are found, then sort.
- **Assumption:** support both; default radius with an expanding-ring fallback for k-nearest.

**Q3 — "How fresh must a moving entity be — sub-second, or is a few seconds fine?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Sub-second freshness forces a very different, much more expensive design than the ~5-second window most products actually tolerate."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If SUB-SECOND → push updates over persistent connections and skip durable storage on the hot path. If ~5s → batch and absorb pings into an in-memory index with periodic flush — far cheaper.
- **Assumption:** ~5 seconds is acceptable (matches Uber/Tinder reality).

**Q4 — "What's the read volume and the moving-entity count, roughly? Global or single-region?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "The ratio of writes to reads decides the whole architecture — if writes dwarf reads, the firehose is the story; if reads dwarf writes, caching is the story."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If WRITE-HEAVY → in-memory geo-index sharded by cell, async durability. If READ-HEAVY → aggressive query caching, many read replicas.
- **Assumption:** global, ~300M users, ~3M moving entities online, write-heavy (I'll prove this in §3).

**Q5 — "Do results need to be strongly consistent, or is eventual fine for display?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "A nearby list is a snapshot in time — nobody's transferring money — so I'd lean eventual and spend the saved budget on latency."
- ↔️ **Design fork:** If STRONG → read-from-primary, slower, costlier. If EVENTUAL → read replicas + cache, fast and cheap.
- **Assumption:** eventual consistency for search results; the one exception is read-your-own-location-write for a moving entity (covered in §8).

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "I'm going to assume a moving-heavy, eventually-consistent, global service with a five-second freshness window and both radius and k-nearest. If you'd rather pin it to pure-static Yelp, say so and I'll simplify the write path away — otherwise I'll keep going."

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I computed these by writing and running a `capacity.py` — only the rounded results are below. The point isn't precision; it's finding the *one* number that forces a design choice.

*(Glosses: **QPS** = queries per second, how many requests hit the server each second. **DAU** = daily active users. **Shard** = one slice of the data on its own machine. **Replica** = a read-only copy of a shard.)*

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| DAU | 300M users x 10% open/day | 30M | "thirty million active a day" | sizes everything downstream |
| Peak read QPS | 30M DAU x 5 searches ÷ 86,400s ≈ 1.7K/s avg; x4 peak | ~6.9K/s | "about seven thousand searches a second at peak" | reads are *small* — caching easily covers them |
| Cache-miss QPS | 6.9K/s x (1 − 0.80 hit) | ~1.4K/s | "fourteen hundred misses a second hit the index" | warm path needs barely any replicas |
| Cold-cache reads | 6.9K/s at 0% hit | ~6.9K/s | "seven thousand a second worst case" | one index replica still covers it (~8K/s each) |
| **Moving-entity write QPS** | 3M online ÷ 4s ping interval | **~750K/s** | **"three quarters of a million location updates a second"** | **the binding bottleneck — writes dwarf reads** |
| Write : read ratio | 750K ÷ 6.9K | ~108 : 1 | "a hundred writes for every read" | spend the budget on the WRITE path, not reads |
| **Hot dense-cell QPS (one shard)** | writes 750K x 4% = 30K/s + reads 6.9K x 4% = 0.3K/s | **~30K/s** | **"thirty thousand a second on one city shard"** | **the decision-forcing number — see below** |
| Static places storage | 50M places x 1KB x 3 replicas | ~0.15TB | "a couple hundred gigs, a non-issue" | storage is *not* the constraint |
| Live-location storage | 3M x 100B in memory | ~0.3GB | "fits in RAM trivially" | keep live dots in memory, not on disk |

**The ONE decision-forcing number:** the **hottest dense-city geo-cell carries ~30K ops/s of combined writes-plus-reads on the single shard that owns it.** A single shard (a Redis or sharded-DB node) tops out around **~50K ops/s**. So today we're at ~0.6x of one shard's ceiling — survivable on one node, *barely*.

**Flip threshold:** the moment a single cell's combined QPS crosses **~50K/s** (e.g. New Year's Eve in Manhattan, or a city's online-driver count doubles), that cell can no longer live on one shard. That triggers **dynamic re-sharding** — splitting the hot cell into finer sub-cells across multiple shards, plus a read-replica and a per-cell cache. This is the hot-key problem and it's deep dive #2.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that surprised me is the write-to-read ratio — it's about a hundred to one. We get maybe seven thousand searches a second at peak, which is small and caches away to almost nothing, but three million moving entities pinging every four seconds is three quarters of a million writes a second. So this is a *write* problem, not a read problem, and that flips the usual instinct. The single number I'll design around is the hottest city cell at about thirty thousand ops a second — one shard can do fifty thousand, so we're fine until a city gets hot enough to cross that line, and then we split the cell."

---

## 4. Core Entities

*(First draft — full field definitions land in §7.)*

**Place / Entity** — a point on the map: a static business *or* a moving person/driver. Owns its identity, location, category, and (for places) metadata like rating and hours.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a pin on a paper map — except some pins are glued down (restaurants) and some slide around (drivers)."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you avoid re-indexing a moving pin on every tiny GPS jitter?" → "We only re-bucket when it crosses a *cell* boundary; sub-cell jitter just updates the in-memory coordinate."

**Geo-Cell** — the unit of spatial partitioning: a fixed region of the Earth's surface (a geohash prefix, an S2 cell, or a quadtree node). Owns the *set of entity IDs currently inside it*. This is the shard key.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a square on a chessboard laid over the map — to find nearby things you only look at your square and its neighbours, never the whole board."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Fixed-size cells waste space in the desert and overflow in cities — how do you handle that?" → "Variable-resolution cells: subdivide dense cells finer (quadtree/S2), so each cell holds a bounded number of entities."

**Location Update** — an event: entity X is now at (lat, lng) at time T. Ephemeral, high-volume, flows through the write firehose.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a heartbeat — 'I'm here now' — that we mostly keep in memory and forget quickly."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Do you store every ping forever?" → "No — the *current* position lives in memory; we sample a fraction to durable storage for history/analytics, the rest is fire-and-forget."

**Search Query** — a request: from (lat, lng), give me entities within radius R or the k nearest, filtered (category, open-now) and ranked.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "a flashlight beam from where you stand — it lights up only what's in range."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Radius results can be cached but moving entities go stale — how?" → "Cache *static-place* queries with a short TTL; for moving entities, read the live in-memory index, never the cache."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The entities are a Place-or-Entity pin, the Geo-Cell that buckets them — that's the shard key and the most important concept here — a high-volume Location Update event, and the Search Query. The surprising one is the Geo-Cell: it's not really 'data', it's the *partitioning scheme*, and almost every hard decision in this design is really a decision about how we define and size those cells. I'll flesh out the fields and the indexes in the data model."

---

## 5. API / Interface

Default REST over HTTPS. The caller's identity always comes from the JWT *(JSON Web Token — a signed token proving who you are, so the server never trusts a client-supplied user id)*; the client never sends its own user id, and never sends a price or a rating it could forge.

### FR1+FR2 — Search nearby (read)

**`GET /v1/nearby`** — GET because it only reads; safe and cacheable.

Key params:
- `lat`, `lng: float` — the searcher's position. *(Note: even this we sanity-check server-side against the JWT's recent locations to limit spoofing.)*
- `radius_m: int` *or* `k: int` — radius in metres, or k-nearest. One or the other.
- `type: enum` — `place` or `person`; routes to the static index vs the live index.
- `category, open_now: optional` — ranking/filter hints.

```
// Request
GET /v1/nearby?lat=40.7580&lng=-73.9855&radius_m=1000&type=place&category=coffee&open_now=true

// Response 200
{
  "results": [
    { "id": "plc_88a1", "name": "Blue Bottle", "distance_m": 120, "rating": 4.6, "open": true },
    { "id": "plc_2f0c", "name": "Stumptown",   "distance_m": 340, "rating": 4.4, "open": true }
  ],
  "cell_resolution": 7,
  "next_cursor": "eyJjZWxsIjoiZHIzMnAifQ"
}
```
- **Key design decision:** results are *paged by expanding cell ring* via `next_cursor`, not a global offset — page 2 means "next ring of cells out", which is cheap and stable as entities move. `distance_m` is computed server-side (exact haversine) after the coarse cell filter — the cell scan is approximate, the final sort is exact.

### FR3 — Update a moving entity's location (write firehose)

**`POST /v1/locations`** — POST because it creates/overwrites state; idempotent on (entity_id, ts).

Key fields:
- `lat, lng: float`, `ts: epoch_ms` — where and when. `ts` is the **idempotency / ordering key**: a ping with an older `ts` than the last seen is dropped (out-of-order GPS).
- `entity_id` is **absent** — taken from the JWT, so a driver can't move someone else's pin.

```
// Request
POST /v1/locations
{ "lat": 40.7142, "lng": -74.0060, "ts": 1718960000123 }

// Response 202
{ "accepted": true, "cell": "dr5regw3", "stale_dropped": false }
```
- **Key design decision:** returns **202 Accepted**, not 200 — the write is queued/applied async to the in-memory index, not durably committed before responding. This is what makes a firehose affordable. `stale_dropped` tells a client its out-of-order ping was ignored.

### FR4 — Register / update a static place (low-volume write)

**`PUT /v1/places/{id}`** — PUT because it fully replaces the place record; naturally idempotent.

```
// Request
PUT /v1/places/plc_88a1
{ "name": "Blue Bottle", "lat": 40.7581, "lng": -73.9856, "category": "coffee", "hours": "07-19" }

// Response 200
{ "id": "plc_88a1", "indexed_in_cell": "dr5regw3p", "version": 12 }
```
- **Key design decision:** the write goes to the durable store first, then asynchronously propagates into the geo-index and ranking cache via CDC *(Change Data Capture — a stream of "row X changed" events tailing the DB)*. `version` enables optimistic concurrency for concurrent business edits.

> 💬 "Notice the moving-entity write returns 202 and the search computes exact distance server-side — the client gets a coarse-then-exact answer it can't tamper with."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Three endpoints. A GET for nearby search — it does a coarse cell scan then an exact haversine sort, and pages by expanding the cell ring outward. A POST for location updates that returns 202 because we apply it asynchronously to an in-memory index — that's the only way three quarters of a million writes a second is affordable. And a PUT for static places that lands in the durable DB first and trickles into the index via change-data-capture. The security thread through all of them: identity comes from the JWT, never the request body, so nobody moves someone else's pin or forges a rating."

## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time)

Shared palette across all diagrams: blue = clients, green = our services, amber = async/queues, grey = datastores, purple = external.

### 6.1 — Search nearby within a radius (FR1) + Rank (FR2)

The interesting bit: turn "distance to everything" into "scan a few cells, hydrate, sort." FR2 (ranking) folds in at the end of this same flow, so I draw them together.

**Architecture decisions**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Search Service | Stateless service that turns a (lat,lng,radius) into a set of geo-cells, scans them, then ranks | Owns the geo-math + ranking in one hop to hit the 200ms budget | Not doing geo-math in the DB query layer — couples query logic to one store and blocks caching | Must keep the cell-cover logic and the store in sync |
| Geo-Index | A spatial index mapping cell → entity IDs; S2 cells over Redis sorted sets / a geo-DB | Lets a query touch ~9 cells instead of 50M rows | Not SQL with lat/lng columns + BETWEEN — scans every row, no spatial pruning | Approximate at cell edges; exact distance re-checked after |
| Ranking Store | Holds the rich fields (rating, hours, popularity) keyed by entity id | Geo-index holds only IDs+coords; we hydrate the top candidates here | Not stuffing all metadata into the geo-index — bloats it and slows scans | One extra lookup per query (batched) |
| Query Cache | Redis cache of (cell, category, radius) → result page, short TTL | 80% of searches are popular city/category combos — absorb them | Not caching per-exact-latlng — cache never hits; we bucket to cell | Static-place results only; moving entities bypass it |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "The Search Service is stateless and owns the geo-math — it figures out which cells cover your circle, scans the Geo-Index for the entity IDs in those cells, then hydrates the rich fields from the Ranking Store and sorts. I picked an S2-cell geo-index over plain lat/lng columns because a SQL BETWEEN on two columns still scans every row — the spatial index lets me touch about nine cells instead of fifty million rows. I keep the geo-index lean — just IDs and coordinates — and hydrate ratings and hours separately, because fattening the index slows every scan. And I cache popular city-category queries with a short TTL, but only for static places; moving dots always read live."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **S2 cell** — Google's scheme that maps the Earth's curved surface onto a flat grid of cells using a space-filling curve, so nearby points get nearby cell IDs. Here it's the spatial index that lets a query touch a handful of cells.
> **Geohash** — encodes a lat/lng into a short string where a shared prefix means physical nearness. The simple alternative to S2; great for radius, weaker at cell-edge neighbours.
> **Quadtree** — a tree that recursively splits a region into four quadrants, subdividing only where points are dense. Here it's the variable-resolution alternative that solves dense-city overflow.
> **Haversine** — the formula for great-circle distance between two lat/lng points. Here it's the *exact* distance we compute after the coarse cell filter, to sort precisely.
> **TTL (time-to-live)** — how long a cached value stays valid before it expires. Here a few-second TTL keeps cached place results fresh enough.

**Diagram — nearby search + rank**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client[User app]:::client
    Client -->|"1 GET nearby latlng radius"| Search
    subgraph ReadPath["Read path"]
        Search[Search Service]:::svc
        Search -->|"2 check cache"| Cache[(Query Cache)]:::store
        Search -->|"3 scan covering cells"| Geo[(Geo-Index)]:::store
        Search -->|"4 hydrate top candidates"| Rank[(Ranking Store)]:::store
    end
    Cache -.->|"2b hit returns page"| Search
    Search -->|"5 exact sort and return"| Client
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
```
↳ reuses existing: none yet — this is the first slice.

**Numbered narration:**
1. **Client → Search Service (`GET /v1/nearby`).** Why here: the Search Service owns the cell-cover math. State: a request in flight. What could go wrong: malformed lat/lng → 400 with validation.
2. **Search → Query Cache.** Why: 80% of city-category queries repeat; a hit skips the index entirely. State: on hit (2b) we return immediately; on miss continue. Risk: stale cache → short TTL bounds staleness to a few seconds.
3. **Search → Geo-Index (scan covering cells).** Why: the index prunes 50M points to the ~9 cells covering the radius. State: a candidate ID list. Risk: cell-edge points just outside radius are over-included → fixed by step 5's exact filter.
4. **Search → Ranking Store (hydrate).** Why: the index only knows IDs+coords; ratings/hours live here. State: candidates now carry rich fields. Risk: a missing record → drop it from results, log.
5. **Search exact-sorts (haversine) and returns.** Why: cell scan is approximate; final sort must be exact. State: response sent, page cached. Risk: too many candidates → cap and page via cursor.

> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "The whole trick is right here — we never compute distance to everything; we let the cell index pick the neighbourhood, then do exact math only on that short list."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "A search comes in, the Search Service checks the query cache for that city-and-category, and on a miss it asks the geo-index which entities sit in the cells covering your circle. That returns IDs and coordinates only, so it hydrates ratings and hours from the ranking store, does the exact haversine sort, returns a page, and caches it briefly. The one decision that makes this work is keeping the index lean and hydrating separately — it keeps scans fast and lets the cache absorb the popular queries."

### 6.2 — Update a moving entity's location (FR3) — the write firehose

This is the binding bottleneck (108:1 writes). The whole slice exists to absorb 750K writes/s cheaply while keeping the index ~5s fresh.

**Architecture decisions**

| Component | What it is | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Location Ingest | Stateless fleet terminating the firehose, validates + dedups by ts | Horizontally scalable front door; absorbs spiky pings | Not writing pings straight to a DB — 750K/s of disk writes is unaffordable | Pings are async, not durable on ack |
| Update Stream | Kafka topic partitioned by geo-cell, buffering location events | Decouples ingest spikes from index apply; preserves per-cell order | Not direct synchronous index writes — couples ingest to index availability | Adds ~1-2s of pipeline latency (within 5s budget) |
| Live Geo-Index | In-memory (Redis) index of current positions per cell | RAM is the only thing fast enough for 750K updates/s; live data is only ~0.3GB | Not on-disk index — can't sustain write rate | Volatile — rebuilt from stream on restart |
| Location Sampler | Worker that samples ~5% of pings to durable history | History/analytics without paying to store every ping | Not storing all pings — that's terabytes/day of write-once data | Lossy history (fine — it's analytics, not source of truth) |

> 🎙️ **Narration:** "A driver pings every four seconds, so I terminate that on a stateless Location Ingest fleet that just validates and drops out-of-order pings, then drops the event onto a Kafka topic partitioned by geo-cell. Kafka — a durable append-only log that buffers a firehose so a slow consumer never loses data — decouples the spiky ingest from the index. Consumers apply each ping to an in-memory Redis index keyed by cell, because RAM is the only thing fast enough for three-quarters-of-a-million writes a second, and the live data is only a few hundred megs so it fits easily. I sample five percent of pings to durable storage for history; storing all of them would be terabytes a day for no product value."

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Kafka** — a durable, append-only log that buffers a stream of events so nothing is lost if a consumer falls behind. Here it absorbs the location firehose and preserves per-cell ordering.
> **Partition (Kafka)** — a slice of a topic that one consumer reads in order. Here we partition by geo-cell so all of one cell's updates stay ordered on one consumer.
> **In-memory index** — data structures held in RAM, not on disk, for microsecond access. Here it holds the live positions because disk can't take the write rate.
> **Idempotent by ts** — applying the same or an older update twice has no effect because we keep only the newest timestamp. Here it makes retried/out-of-order pings safe.

**Diagram — location update firehose**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Driver[Driver app]:::client
    Driver -->|"1 POST location"| Ingest
    subgraph WritePath["Write firehose"]
        Ingest[Location Ingest]:::svc
        Ingest -->|"2 publish by cell"| Stream[[Update Stream]]:::async
        Stream -->|"3 consume per cell"| Applier[Index Applier]:::svc
    end
    Applier -->|"4 upsert position"| Live[(Live Geo-Index)]:::store
    Stream -.->|"5 sample 5pct"| Sampler[Location Sampler]:::svc
    Sampler -.->|"6 append history"| History[(Location History)]:::store
    classDef client fill:#1a3a5c,stroke:#4A90D9,color:#e6edf3
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
```
↳ reuses existing: the Live Geo-Index is the moving-entity counterpart of §6.1's Geo-Index.

**Numbered narration:**
1. **Driver → Ingest (`POST /v1/locations`, 202).** Why: a thin stateless tier scales to the firehose. State: ping accepted. Risk: spoofed entity_id → blocked, id comes from JWT.
2. **Ingest → Update Stream (partition by cell).** Why: Kafka buffers spikes and keeps per-cell order. State: event durably logged. Risk: Kafka partition down → ingest buffers briefly, then sheds with backpressure.
3. **Stream → Index Applier (consume).** Why: applier owns index mutation. State: consumed. Risk: lag spike → freshness slips past 5s → alarm on consumer lag.
4. **Applier → Live Geo-Index (upsert, idempotent by ts).** Why: in-memory is the only thing fast enough. State: entity's current cell updated; if it crossed a boundary it's removed from the old cell. Risk: out-of-order ping → dropped by ts check.
5–6. **Stream → Sampler → History (5%).** Why: cheap analytics trail. State: durable sample written. Risk: sampler down → we lose *history* only, live path unaffected.

> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "The 202 is the whole point — we acknowledge fast and apply async, because synchronously durably writing 750K pings a second would bankrupt us."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Location updates are the firehose, so the design is all about absorbing them cheaply. Ingest terminates the connection and acks with a 202, the ping rides a Kafka topic partitioned by cell, and an applier upserts it into the in-memory Redis index — dropping anything out of order by timestamp. We sample a small fraction to durable history and throw the rest away. The trade-off I accept is that an ack doesn't mean durable — but for live location that's exactly right, the next ping is four seconds away anyway."

### 6.3 — Register / update a static place (FR4)

Low volume, but it must reach both the geo-index and the ranking store consistently.

**Architecture decisions**

| Component | What it is | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Place Service | Service handling business CRUD, writes to durable DB | Static places need a durable source of truth (the DB) | Not writing directly to the in-memory index — it's volatile, places must survive restarts | Index lags the DB by CDC latency (seconds) |
| Places DB | Durable relational/document store, source of truth for places | ACID for a clean business record; low write volume | Not Redis — places are durable, not ephemeral | An extra propagation hop into the index |
| CDC Pipeline | Tails the DB change-log and emits "place changed" events | Keeps geo-index + ranking + cache in sync without dual-writes | Not dual-writing from the app — risks half-applied writes | Eventual: index trails DB by seconds |

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — a stream of "row X changed" events read off the database's write-ahead log. Here it propagates place edits into the index and cache without the app dual-writing.
> **Source of truth** — the one authoritative copy of the data. Here the Places DB is authoritative; the index is a derived, rebuildable view.

**Numbered narration:**
1. **Owner → Place Service (`PUT`).** Why: owns business record validation. State: request validated. Risk: bad geo → reject.
2. **Place → Places DB.** Why: durable source of truth. State: row committed with new version. Risk: DB down → 503, retried.
3–6. **CDC fans out** to geo-index (re-bucket if location changed), ranking store (rating/hours), and cache (invalidate the affected cell's pages). State: derived views catch up within seconds. Risk: CDC lag → stale place briefly; bounded and alarmed.

> 💬 **Say-while-drawing:** "One write, one source of truth, and CDC fans it out — so we never dual-write and risk a half-indexed place."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Static places are the easy write — low volume but they must be durable, so they land in a real database that's the source of truth, and a change-data-capture stream fans each edit out to the geo-index, the ranking store, and the cache. I deliberately don't have the app write to all three itself, because dual-writes are where you get a place that's in the index but not in the ranking store. The cost is a few seconds of lag, which is fine for a new restaurant showing up."
